title: Managing Khaki Campbell ducks for eggs
subtitle: Housing, feeding, and daily routine for the UK's most productive duck.
excerpt: A Khaki Campbell duck can outlay a hybrid hen in a good season, some strains reliably produce over 300 eggs a year. They're easier to keep than you might expect, but they need a few things that hens don't: standing water to wash their bills, a pop-hole with a ramp rather than a ladder, and a morning routine that works with their early-laying habit.
sourceNotes: Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, waterfowl chapters. DEFRA/APHA Poultry Welfare Code. Cornell Small Farms duck husbandry guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A male duck; not needed for egg production but required for fertile eggs and hatching.
The broad, flat beak of a duck; ducks rinse and clean their nostrils by submerging the bill in water, making a drinking trough deeper than a chicken's drinker essential.

content.text: Khaki Campbell ducks are the reliable commercial egg duck of the UK: light-bodied, hardy, good foragers, and capable of laying most days of the year through their second and third seasons. They don't need a pond, a rubber tub deep enough to submerge their
satisfies their welfare need. They do need a different setup to hens, and the morning routine matters more than it does with chickens.

Ducks sleep on the floor, not on perches. A house with 0.1 m² of floor space per duck, dry bedding of straw or shavings, and a low pop-hole with a gentle ramp rather than a step is all they need. They're more cold-hardy than hens but less tolerant of draughts, ventilation at the roof, not at floor level.

Ducks need water deep enough to submerge their bills and nostrils, a standard chicken drinker is not adequate. A rubber tub at least 10 cm deep and wide enough for the duck to dip its whole head is the minimum. They will spill water continuously. Site the tub on a raised slatted platform or gravel pad so the surrounding area doesn't become a mud trap. Refresh the water twice daily; ducks contaminate water faster than hens.

Feed a laying duck pellet formulated for ducks, not hens, the niacin levels differ and hens' layers pellets are deficient for ducks over time. Supplement with flint grit (not oyster shell, which is too coarse for ducks). Ducks are effective grazers and slug-eaters, so access to grass in season reduces pellet consumption.

Khaki Campbells lay in the early morning, usually before 9 am. Keep them in the house until after 9 am every day and collect the eggs before letting them out, a duck that starts laying in the run lays there every day afterwards. Once the laying habit is established in the house, they can be let out earlier.

is not needed for egg production. For a laying-only flock, keep ducks only. One drake can service up to six ducks, but his presence can stress a small flock, a single drake with three ducks will over-mate them to the point of feather damage on the neck and back.

attrs.cue: Eggs found scattered around the run rather than in the house
attrs.cause: Ducks locked out of the house before 9 am laying window, or run access starts too early
attrs.fix: Delay opening the pop-hole until 9,9:30 am for the first few weeks to establish the house-laying habit.

attrs.cue: Ducks with discharge from nostrils, head-shaking, or difficulty breathing
attrs.cause: Access to deep water insufficient; nostrils blocked
attrs.fix: Provide a tub deep enough for full bill submersion. Ducks that can't clear their nostrils in water develop respiratory problems.
